explanation: The sentence states: 'A generic DMj that prop_0, will prop_1. This property holds for every DMi, DMj, and DMk.' In LTL, this is interpreted as: globally (always), if prop_0 ever holds, then prop_1 must also hold (here, in the next step or possibly at the same time depending on the intent, but typically 'will' means either the same moment or after). Since no next is stated, we'll use implication at the same time. Since this should hold for any entity and only prop_0/prop_1 are given, we express this as G(prop_0 -> prop_1).

The natural language states that whenever prop_0 holds (for a generic DMj), then prop_1 will hold (for that DMj). Since the property must hold for all DMj (and other indices DMi, DMk), and since we only use atomic propositions without indexing, the translation is: globally, if prop_0 then eventually prop_1.

The statement says that for every DMj such that prop_0 holds, prop_1 will also hold. Since it says the property holds for every DMi, DMj, and DMk, it's universally quantified. In LTL, this is typically represented as: globally, if prop_0 then prop_1, i.e., at every point in time, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold. This captures the stated relationship without including numerical comparison operators.

The statement says if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 will hold as well, with some context that these should be generic (i.e., quantified over all possible indices, but in LTL with atomic props only, we ignore indices). The statement 'will prop_1' is interpreted as 'eventually prop_1'. Since this property holds for every DMx, we simply translate the implication globally: "If prop_0 holds, then eventually prop_1 holds" (globally over the trace).

The sentence says: For any entity DMj, if prop_0 happens, then prop_1 will happen. The note, 'For every DMi, DMj, and DMk,' means this must be universally true regardless of entity, so we express the implication globally, with the understood context that prop_0 and prop_1 are already made generic per-entity. In LTL, we express 'whenever prop_0 happens, prop_1 will happen' as 'Globally (prop_0 -> prop_1)'.

The statement means: whenever prop_0 holds, eventually prop_1 must hold, and this must be true globally. There is no timing constraint, so we use a global implication from prop_0 to 'eventually prop_1'. The universal quantification over DMi, DMj, and DMk means this logic applies for every assignment, but in LTL with atomic propositions the formula will not explicitly reference these universally quantified variables and instead will just be an implication.

The phrase 'a generic DMj that prop_0, will prop_1' expresses that whenever 'prop_0' holds, then inevitably 'prop_1' will also hold, for any DMj. Given this must be true for all DMi, DMj, DMk (i.e., universally for any agent), it suffices to state that globally, if 'prop_0' holds, then eventually 'prop_1' holds. In LTL: G(prop_0 -> F prop_1).

The sentence expresses: whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 will hold. This requirement is meant to be general for any indices, but since only prop_0 and prop_1 are available as atomic propositions and no indices or variables are present in LTL, the universal aspect reduces to a simple implication globally: whenever prop_0 happens, then eventually prop_1 will happen.

The phrase "prop_0 will prop_1" in LTL suggests that whenever prop_0 holds, eventually prop_1 will hold (i.e., implication with an 'eventually' operator: globally, if prop_0 holds, then eventually prop_1 will hold). The statement also says this property must hold for all generic DMi, DMj, DMk, which in LTL just ensures the property is general and universally applied, assuming the atomic propositions already refer to the DMs. Thus, we translate as: 'Globally, whenever prop_0 holds, eventually prop_1 must hold.'

The statement is generic and refers to propositions prop_0 and prop_1 holding for every DMi, DMj, and DMk. In the absence of explicit order or further temporal detail, the most natural translation is that both prop_0 and prop_1 are always true (globally hold) over all time. If the generic 'DM' variables suggest quantification over different components, that would correspond to universal quantification, which LTL alone does not handle over indices, so we interpret this as 'it is globally (always) the case that prop_0 and prop_1 hold'.
